ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for soursop iced drink; closest parent is fruit juice food product.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (Fruit nectars and fruit drinks) is the closest defensible parent; no soursop-specific code exists.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for iced soursop drink.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 250 g (one glass), typical of a Malaysian hawker or café iced fruit drink portion.

nutrient_source.title: Soursop, raw (Annona muricata) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, FDC ID 167762
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are based on raw soursop pulp per USDA SR Legacy (FDC ID 167762). The iced drink is prepared by blending soursop pulp with water and ice; values are adapted to reflect an approximate 60% soursop pulp to 40% water dilution typical of a hawker-style iced soursop drink, with a small amount of added sugar (~5 g per 100 g serving) estimated from common preparation. Energy and macros adjusted accordingly. Sodium reflects natural fruit content plus negligible preparation addition.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for iced soursop drink was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 42 is estimated by analogy to tropical fruit juices with similar sugar composition (fructose-dominant) as reported in Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Soursop pulp contains primarily fructose and glucose; the diluted iced drink preparation lowers effective GI relative to concentrated juice. GL per serving calculated as (42 × 30 g available carbohydrate per 250 g serving) / 100 = 12.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content; naturally rich in potassium
hypertension.guidance: Iced soursop drink is low in sodium (~10 mg per 100 g) and provides potassium (~170 mg per 100 g), making it a suitable occasional beverage for individuals managing hypertension, though added sugar should be minimised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate sugar content and glycemic load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 42 (low) but a glycemic load of ~12.6 per 250 g serving, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size and avoid versions with added sugar to prevent postprandial glucose spikes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat; no cholesterol; contains vitamin C and potassium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Iced soursop drink is very low in saturated fat and cholesterol-free, and provides vitamin C and potassium which support vascular health; however, regular consumption of sweetened versions should be moderated due to free sugar content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Iced Soursop Drink (also known as Iced Sour Sop or Air Sirsak Ais) is a popular chilled fruit beverage made from the pulp of the soursop fruit (Annona muricata), blended or mixed with water, ice, and optionally sugar or syrup. It is widely consumed across Malaysia, Singapore, and Southeast Asia as a refreshing hawker-style drink. The soursop fruit has a distinctive flavour profile that is simultaneously sweet, tangy, and slightly creamy, often described as a combination of strawberry, pineapple, and citrus with a custard-like undertone. The drink is typically pale white to off-white in colour and has a smooth, slightly pulpy texture. Nutritionally, iced soursop drink provides moderate carbohydrates primarily from natural fruit sugars (fructose and glucose), with a low glycemic index of approximately 42, making it a relatively better choice among fruit drinks for glycemic management when unsweetened. It is a source of vitamin C, potassium, and B vitamins including thiamine and riboflavin. The drink is naturally low in fat, sodium, and protein. Clinically, it is suitable for most adults in moderate portions; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be cautious of added sugar versions. It is vegan, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free, making it broadly accessible across dietary preferences in Singapore and Malaysia.
common_user_queries: Is soursop drink good for diabetics?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for iced soursop drink was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy raw soursop pulp data (FDC ID 167762) with dilution adjustment for the iced drink preparation. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed international GI tables. Confidence is medium rather than low because the adaptation methodology is transparent and the source data quality is high.
notes: Nutrient values adapted from USDA SR Legacy raw soursop (FDC ID 167762) with approximately 60:40 soursop-to-water dilution ratio and ~5 g/100 g added sugar adjustment typical of hawker-style preparation. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — naturally absent in fruit-based beverages). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant-based food contains no B12). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — soursop fruit contains no vitamin D). selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for soursop. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant-based beverage). GI of 42 is an analogous estimate based on tropical fruit juice GI values from Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables; no direct soursop drink GI measurement was identified in the University of Sydney GI Database. GL per 250 g serving = (42 × 30) / 100 = 12.6. Food originally listed as 'ICED SOUR SOP' on field survey data at The Rainbow Bistro (60 Boat Quay); canonical English name normalised to 'Iced Soursop Drink'. Cuisine tags from source (Curry Rice, Nasi Lemak) appear to be restaurant-level tags unrelated to this specific beverage and have not been carried forward.
